coredata.dc:title: A digital twin–enabled measurement and instrumentation framework for ultra-accurate short-term voltage prediction in lithium-ion batteries for electric vehicle applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sayed, Khairy
ElZohri, Hebatallah H.
Abo-Khalil, Ahmed G.
Aref, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: Accurate short-term voltage prediction is essential for safety, efficiency, and optimal power management in electric vehicle (EV) battery systems. However, traditional battery management systems (BMSs) suffer from modeling inaccuracies, sensor drift, low-bandwidth measurements, and limited adaptability to transient load conditions and ageing effects. This research proposes a novel Digital Twin (DT)–enabled measurement and instrumentation framework that integrates high-frequency sensing, adaptive sensor calibration, and hybrid physics–artificial intelligence (AI) modeling to achieve ultra-accurate lithium-ion battery voltage prediction. The proposed system incorporates a real-time DT combining an equivalent circuit model, electro-thermal ageing representation, and a deep learning prediction layer. A multisensor instrumentation platform—comprising high-resolution voltage and current measurement, temperature arrays, and low-amplitude impedance probing—continuously synchronizes the DT with physical battery behavior through advanced sensor-fusion algorithms. An AI-based short-term forecasting module (long short-term memory/temporal convolutional network/physics-informed neural network) predicts terminal voltage 50–500 ms ahead, enabling proactive power management under dynamic EV driving conditions. Experimental validation using real driving cycles demonstrates significant improvements in prediction accuracy, state of charge estimation, and robustness against sensor drift compared to conventional BMS approaches. This work provides a foundation for next-generation predictive BMS architectures, enhancing EV safety, performance, and battery longevity through intelligent DT–assisted instrumentation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Digital twin
Battery systems
Battery management systems
Electro-thermal ageing

Hybrid physics–AI short-term voltage prediction model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Digital twin-enabled measurement for short-term voltage prediction in Li-ion batteries. LSTM = long short-term memory; PINN = physics-informed neural network; TCN = temporal convolutional network.

Battery prediction methodology in digital twin. AI = artificial intelligence; ADC = analog-to-digital converter; DT =Digital Twin; EKF = extended Kalman filter; LSTM = long short-term memory; MAE = mean absolute error; OCV = open-circuit voltage; PINN = physics-informed neural network; RMSE = root mean squared error; SEI = solid electrolyte interphase; TCN = temporal convolutional network; UKF = unscented Kalman filter.

Predictive battery voltage estimation. LSTM = long short-term memory; MAE = mean absolute error; PINN = physics-informed neural network; RMSE = root mean squared error; TCN = temporal convolutional network.
Diagnostic flowchart for prediction deviations. SEI = solid electrolyte interphase.
block diagram of hardware setup. ADC = analog-to-digital converter; BMS = battery management system.
Battery digital twin voltage prediction results. MAE = mean absolute error; RMSE = root mean squared error.
Digital twin-enabled SOC prediction. SOC = state of charge.
Digital twin vs simple ECM prediction. ECM = equivalent circuit model.

head.ce:title: A digital twin–enabled measurement and instrumentation framework for ultra-accurate short-term voltage prediction in lithium-ion batteries for electric vehicle applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate short-term voltage prediction is essential for safety, efficiency, and optimal power management in electric vehicle (EV) battery systems. However, traditional battery management systems (BMSs) suffer from modeling inaccuracies, sensor drift, low-bandwidth measurements, and limited adaptability to transient load conditions and ageing effects. This research proposes a novel Digital Twin (DT)–enabled measurement and instrumentation framework that integrates high-frequency sensing, adaptive sensor calibration, and hybrid physics–artificial intelligence (AI) modeling to achieve ultra-accurate lithium-ion battery voltage prediction. The proposed system incorporates a real-time DT combining an equivalent circuit model, electro-thermal ageing representation, and a deep learning prediction layer. A multisensor instrumentation platform—comprising high-resolution voltage and current measurement, temperature arrays, and low-amplitude impedance probing—continuously synchronizes the DT with physical battery behavior through advanced sensor-fusion algorithms. An AI-based short-term forecasting module (long short-term memory/temporal convolutional network/physics-informed neural network) predicts terminal voltage 50–500 ms ahead, enabling proactive power management under dynamic EV driving conditions. Experimental validation using real driving cycles demonstrates significant improvements in prediction accuracy, state of charge estimation, and robustness against sensor drift compared to conventional BMS approaches. This work provides a foundation for next-generation predictive BMS architectures, enhancing EV safety, performance, and battery longevity through intelligent DT–assisted instrumentation.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) have become the dominant energy storage technology for modern electric vehicles (EVs) owing to their high energy density, long cycle life, and favorable power characteristics. As EV adoption accelerates and performance expectations continue to rise, batteries are increasingly required to operate under highly dynamic conditions, including rapid acceleration, regenerative braking, and fast charging. Under these scenarios, accurate and timely prediction of battery terminal voltage is essential for ensuring safe, efficient, and reliable vehicle operation [1–5].

Terminal voltage is one of the most informative and readily measurable signals in a (BMS). It serves as a critical indicator for estimating state of charge (SOC), state of health (SOH), thermal condition, power capability, and protection thresholds. However, battery voltage behavior is inherently nonlinear and strongly influenced by coupled electrochemical, thermal, and aging processes. Fast current transients, temperature variations, internal resistance growth, and sensor imperfections further complicate voltage dynamics [6–10]. As a result, accurately predicting short-term voltage behavior—particularly during aggressive driving events—remains a significant challenge.

Conventional BMS architectures primarily rely on simplified equivalent circuit models (ECMs) combined with low-bandwidth sensing hardware. While these models are computationally efficient and suitable for embedded implementation, they struggle to capture rapid voltage fluctuations and nonlinear dynamics under real-world EV duty cycles. Consequently, many EVs operate with conservative power limits, experience inaccuracies in SOC estimation, or encounter unexpected undervoltage events during peak load demands [11–15]. These limitations not only reduce usable energy and vehicle performance but also impact battery longevity and safety.

Battery voltage modeling has been extensively studied due to its central role in BMS design. Early research focused on physics-based and ECM approaches such as the Rint, Thevenin, and dual-polarization models. These models offer real-time computability and reasonable accuracy under steady-state conditions but require frequent parameter identification and exhibit degraded performance under temperature variations and aging effects [16–20]. More advanced electrochemical models, including pseudo-2-dimensional formulations, provide high-fidelity representations of battery behavior but are computationally intensive and unsuitable for real-time implementation on embedded BMS hardware.

To overcome these challenges, data-driven and machine learning (ML) approaches have gained increasing attention. Neural networks, Gaussian process regression, and deep temporal models have demonstrated strong capabilities in capturing nonlinear voltage dynamics. In particular, recurrent neural networks such as long short-term memory (LSTM) and gated recurrent unit (GRU), as well as temporal convolutional networks (TCNs), have shown promising results in short-term voltage forecasting under variable load conditions [21–24]. ML techniques have also been widely applied to battery degradation modeling and SOH estimation, improving long-term monitoring accuracy [25].

Despite these advances, purely data-driven models suffer from several limitations. They often lack physical interpretability, require large and representative training datasets, and may generate unrealistic predictions when operating outside their training domain. These shortcomings have motivated the development of hybrid and physics-informed approaches, such as physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), which embed physical constraints or ECM structures within artificial intelligence (AI) models to enhance robustness and generalization [26–30].

More recently, Digital Twin (DT) technology has emerged as a transformative paradigm for modeling and managing complex cyber–physical systems. A DT is a dynamic virtual representation of a physical system that continuously synchronizes with real-world measurements. In the context of EV batteries, a DT integrates multidomain sensing, physics-based modeling, and AI-driven analytics to replicate battery behavior with high fidelity in real time.

Several studies have demonstrated the potential of DTs for battery lifecycle monitoring, electro-thermal estimation, impedance tracking, fault diagnosis, and safety management [30–35]. By maintaining continuous alignment between the physical battery and its virtual counterpart, a DT can track aging mechanisms, identify sensor drift, and support predictive diagnostics and control. This capability opens the door to next-generation predictive BMS architectures, where voltage dips and power constraints can be anticipated rather than merely reacted to.

However, despite growing interest in battery DTs, existing research has largely focused on state estimation or long-term degradation analysis. The integration of DT technology with advanced measurement and instrumentation systems—specifically for short-term (50–500 ms ahead) voltage prediction under dynamic EV operation—remains limited. In particular, the combined use of high-frequency sensing, adaptive sensor calibration, real-time sensor fusion, and hybrid physics–AI modeling within a unified DT framework has not been sufficiently explored [36–41].

Conventional BMS measurement and instrumentation systems are constrained by sensor noise, drift, limited sampling rates, and modeling simplifications [42–48]. These limitations result in inaccurate voltage prediction during highly dynamic operating conditions, leading to conservative power limiting, reduced energy utilization, and increased risk of voltage-related faults. While AI-based methods improve prediction accuracy, their robustness across varying temperatures, aging states, and transient current spikes remains a concern. Conversely, physics-based models offer interpretability but struggle to adapt to rapidly changing real-world conditions without extensive parameter updates.

To address this challenge, this research proposes a DT–based measurement and instrumentation framework that tightly integrates real-time sensing, adaptive calibration, and hybrid physics–AI modeling (illustrated in Fig. 1). The overarching goal is to design and experimentally validate an innovative DT-enabled system capable of predictive voltage estimation for EV LiB packs.

The specific objectives of this research are:
1.
To develop a high-fidelity DT model that captures the electro-thermal and aging behavior of EV LiBs by combining physics-based and AI-based modeling approaches.
2.
To design an advanced measurement and instrumentation architecture incorporating high-frequency voltage and current sensing, distributed temperature measurements, and impedance-based diagnostic probing for real-time DT synchronization.
3.
To implement a hybrid sensor-fusion and state-estimation framework (e.g., Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)/Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) combined with AI regression) for adaptive calibration and mitigation of sensor noise, drift, and transient disturbances.
4.
To develop a short-term voltage prediction module using advanced AI techniques (LSTM, TCN, or PINN) constrained by physical battery behavior, enabling voltage forecasting 50–500 ms ahead.
5.
To experimentally validate the proposed DT framework under realistic EV driving cycles, temperature profiles, and battery aging states, and to benchmark its performance against conventional BMS prediction methods.
6.
To quantitatively assess improvements in voltage prediction accuracy, SOC/SOH estimation, and power-limit optimization achieved through the proposed DT-enabled measurement system.

By unifying high-bandwidth instrumentation with a synchronized DT architecture, this research aims to bridge the gap between measurement, modeling, and prediction in EV BMS design. The proposed framework advances the state of the art by enabling millisecond-level voltage forecasting under real-world operating conditions, addressing a critical limitation of current EV battery management solutions (illustrated conceptually in Fig. 2).

This section presents the development of the proposed DT–enabled instrumentation and voltage prediction framework for LiBs in EV applications. The methodology consists of 5 major components: (i) DT modeling, (ii) advanced measurement and instrumentation design, (iii) sensor fusion and adaptive calibration, (iv) hybrid physics–AI short-term voltage prediction, and (v) experimental validation. The overall workflow integrates real-time data acquisition, model synchronization, and predictive analytics to achieve ultra-accurate voltage forecasts under dynamic EV driving conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of the battery digital twin
ce:section.ce:para: The Battery DT is developed as a high-fidelity virtual representation of the physical LiB cell or module, designed to continuously mirror its electrical, thermal, and degradation behavior in real time. The DT architecture is structured into multiple modeling layers to capture different aspects of battery dynamics. The first layer, Electro-Thermal Physics-Based Modeling, describes the battery’s electrical response and heat generation using an enhanced ECM coupled with a thermal energy balance representation, enabling accurate prediction of voltage and temperature under varying load conditions. The second layer, the Aging and Degradation Model, captures long-term performance evolution by incorporating mechanisms such as internal resistance growth, capacity fade, and degradation processes, including solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation and lithium plating propensity. Together, these layers enable the DT to maintain continuous synchronization with the physical battery, providing reliable state estimation and predictive capability for advanced BMSs.

A reduced-order electrochemical model, or an enhanced ECM, is employed to accurately represent key battery behaviors while maintaining computational efficiency. The model captures the open-circuit voltage (OCV) as a function of SOC, enabling a direct link between energy state and voltage output. Dynamic voltage responses are modeled using an resistance and capacitance (RC) network, which reflects transient polarization effects, while Joule heating and thermal conduction are incorporated to account for internal heat generation and dissipation. Additionally, the model includes temperature-dependent resistance and diffusion parameters, allowing it to adapt to varying thermal conditions and accurately simulate both electrical and thermal dynamics for predictive and diagnostic purposes.

The battery terminal voltage is modeled as:
(1)
V
t
=

OCV

− I

(2)

= −
1

V

+
1
C
i
I ( t )

SOC dynamics describe the temporal evolution of the battery’s available energy relative to its nominal capacity. SOC is influenced by charge/discharge currents, internal resistance, and temperature-dependent losses, and it serves as a key input for both safety and energy management in EV applications. Accurate tracking of SOC dynamics, enhanced through sensor fusion and model-based estimation, enables reliable voltage prediction, optimal power distribution, and protection against over-discharge or over-charge conditions. SOC dynamics is given by:
(3)
dSOC
dt
= −
η I ( t )
Q
nom
where η is coulombic efficiency, and
Q
nom
is nominal capacity

For the thermal model, the energy balance equation quantifies the rate of temperature change in the battery based on heat generation from internal losses and heat exchange with the environment. Temperature-dependent resistance reflects how the battery’s internal resistance varies with temperature, influencing both voltage response and heat generation. Together, these models enable accurate prediction of thermal behavior, ensuring safe operation and improved performance under dynamic load and environmental conditions. For the thermal model, the energy balance equation is expressed as:
(4)
C
th
dT
dt
=
I
2

R

T , SOH
− hA ( T −
T
amb
)

To account for temperature effects, the internal resistance parameters are modeled as temperature-dependent functions, obtained through characterization tests conducted at multiple ambient temperatures. The temperature-dependent resistance is expressed as:
(5)
R
int
T
=
R
ref
exp [ α
T −
T
ref
]

The resistance–temperature relationship is represented using a parametric regression model, enabling the DT to dynamically update resistance values based on real-time temperature measurements from the battery instrumentation layer.
Model parameters are initialized through laboratory characterization and updated in real time via sensor fusion. Fig. 3 shows the battery prediction methodology in DT.

The Aging and Degradation Layer models the long-term evolution of battery performance by incorporating key electrochemical degradation mechanisms that occur during repeated charge–discharge cycles. This layer captures phenomena such as SEI growth, lithium plating propensity, and gradual internal resistance increase, which lead to capacity fade and reduced power capability over time. By integrating these ageing indicators into the DT framework, the model can adapt its parameters to reflect the battery’s health condition, enabling more reliable long-term voltage prediction, improved state estimation, and enhanced diagnostic capability for predictive battery management.

A physics-based degradation model is incorporated to capture the dominant aging mechanisms affecting LiBs over their operational lifetime. The model explicitly simulates SEI layer growth, lithium plating phenomena under fast-charging conditions, and the progressive evolution of internal resistance, together with cumulative capacity fade. By continuously updating these degradation states, the framework preserves voltage prediction accuracy across different operating conditions and battery life-cycle stages, enabling reliable long-term performance assessment and control.

Capacity Fade is expressed as:
(6)
Q
t
=
Q
0
−
k
1
t
−
k
2
N
cycles

The resistance growth equation is given by
(7)

k
4

E
a
RT
)

SOH is determined by:
(8)
SOH
t
=
Q ( t )
Q
0
A lightweight ML surrogate (feedforward network) approximates complex electrochemical interactions to allow real-time operation. It is periodically retrained using incoming sensor data.

A high-bandwidth, multisensor measurement and instrumentation system is developed and tightly synchronized with the DT to ensure accurate real-time representation of battery dynamics. The system incorporates high-resolution electrical sensing using 16–18-bit analog-to-digital converter (ADC)-based voltage sensors alongside Hall-effect or precision shunt-based current sensors, enabling reliable capture of fast voltage and current transients at sampling frequencies in the range of 10–50 kHz. To account for thermal effects, a distributed temperature sensor array composed of multiple thermistors or Resistance Temperature Detectors is deployed around the battery module, allowing real-time thermal gradient mapping and continuous updating of the DT’s electro-thermal states. In addition, an impedance-based diagnostic probing mechanism is integrated, in which a low-amplitude excitation signal (typically a 10–50 mA current pulse) is injected during low-load operating intervals. The resulting voltage response is used to estimate internal resistance, diffusion-related time constants, and impedance phase characteristics. These diagnostic indicators provide critical insight into ageing and degradation mechanisms and enable continuous self-calibration and self-correction of the DT, thereby enhancing the robustness and predictive accuracy of the overall battery management framework.

Small-signal impedance estimation can be expressed as:
(9)
Z
j ω
=
Δ V
j ω
Δ I
j ω

Diffusion time constant estimation:
(10)
τ
d
=
R
d
C
d
These parameters update the DT online.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor fusion and adaptive calibration engine
ce:section.ce:para: Measurement data and DT predictions are fused using a 2-stage estimation framework.

The EKF or UKF is employed to estimate key internal battery states, including SOC, thermal states, and internal resistance. By leveraging the physics-based structure of the DT, the Kalman filter effectively suppresses measurement noise while enforcing physical consistency and system constraints. In the prediction step, the state evolution is propagated using the nonlinear system model as
(15)
x
ˆ

while the subsequent correction step updates the state estimates based on incoming measurements, enabling robust and accurate real-time state tracking under dynamic operating conditions.

Continuously compares real sensor data with DT outputs:
(16)
∆ V =
V

−
V
DT

A reduced-order electrochemical model, or an enhanced ECM, is employed to accurately represent key battery behaviors while maintaining computational efficiency. The model captures the OCV as a function of SOC, enabling a direct link between energy state and voltage output. Dynamic voltage responses are modeled using an RC network, which reflects transient polarization effects, while Joule heating and thermal conduction are incorporated to account for internal heat generation and dissipation. Additionally, the model includes temperature-dependent resistance and diffusion parameters, allowing it to adapt to varying thermal conditions and accurately simulate both electrical and thermal dynamics for predictive and diagnostic purposes. This ensures robust and self-correcting instrumentation.

Voltage residual represents the difference between the measured battery terminal voltage and the voltage predicted by the DT or model-based estimator. Monitoring this residual helps detect modeling inaccuracies, sensor anomalies, or emerging battery degradation phenomena, enabling adaptive correction and improved diagnostic capability.
(17)
∆ V =
V
measured
−
V
DT

Recursive parameter update is performed by continuously adjusting model parameters based on the difference between measured outputs and model predictions. This adaptive process enables the system to track parameter variations caused by temperature changes, operating conditions, and battery ageing, thereby maintaining accurate state estimation and prediction performance.
(18)

+ γ ∆ V
∂ V
∂ θ

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybrid physics–AI short-term voltage prediction model
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed Hybrid Physics–AI Short-Term Voltage Prediction Model integrates a physics-based electro-thermal equivalent circuit with a learning-driven temporal model (e.g., LSTM/TCN) to achieve accurate voltage forecasting in LiBs. The DT provides physically consistent intermediate states, including SOC, internal resistance, and polarization voltages, which are fed into the AI module to capture fast transient behaviors under dynamic EV driving conditions. High-frequency multisensor measurements ensure real-time synchronization, while Kalman filter–based sensor fusion corrects for noise and sensor drift. This hybrid approach balances interpretability with predictive performance, enabling stable short-term voltage prediction (50–500 ms) and providing diagnostic insights into degradation mechanisms and operational anomalies.

Input feature vector is given by:
(19)
z
k
= [
V
k
,
I
k
,
T
k
,
dV
dt
,
dT
dt
,
Z
k
,
SOC
k
,
SOH
k
]

Prior to prediction, the measured voltage, current, and temperature signals are preprocessed through normalization and dynamic feature extraction, including derivatives such as dV/dt, dT/dt, and impedance trend indicators. Sliding time windows of 20–200 ms are employed to capture short-term temporal dependencies relevant to fast battery dynamics. The processed data are then fed into a deep learning–based prediction module, which may adopt an LSTM network, a TCN, or a PINN. These architectures are selected to effectively model transient behavior while preserving temporal continuity and, in the case of PINNs, enforcing underlying physical constraints from the DT.

The model predicts the future battery voltage using a learning function Fθ that maps recent measurement sequences and DT states—such as voltage and temperature—to a short-term forecast V^(t+Δt). In the PINN, additional loss terms are incorporated to enforce physical consistency with battery dynamics, ensuring that the learned predictions remain aligned with electro-thermal model constraints.
(20)
V
ˆ
t + ∆ t
=
F
θ
(
z
t − τ : t
,
V
DT
,
T
DT
)

PINN includes loss terms enforcing physical consistency. Physics-Informed Loss Function (PINN) is expressed by:
(21)
L =
L
data
+ λ
L
physics
(22)
L
physics
=
V
AI
−
V
ECM
2

Prediction accuracy is further enhanced by augmenting the neural network inputs with key states extracted from the DT. Specifically, the DT voltage estimate, thermal state, and ageing state are provided as auxiliary inputs, allowing the learning model to account for instantaneous electro-thermal behavior and long-term degradation effects. This tight coupling enables the network to generate physically consistent and more robust short-term voltage predictions under dynamic operating conditions.

Fig. 4 illustrates the overall workflow of the proposed MATLAB-based DT–enabled voltage prediction framework. The flowchart highlights the sequential processing stages, starting from multisensor data acquisition and preprocessing, followed by feature extraction and sliding-window segmentation. The processed data are then fed into the hybrid prediction module, which combines Kalman filtering and deep learning models to estimate short-term battery voltage. Finally, the predicted voltage is evaluated using multiple accuracy and latency metrics, demonstrating the closed-loop integration between measurement, modeling, and performance assessment.

A LiB module is experimentally evaluated under representative operating conditions, including WLTP and UDDS driving cycles as well as high-power charging (HPC) profiles. The tests are conducted across a wide temperature range from 0 °C to 55 °C and at different ageing stages, namely fresh, mid-life, and aged batteries. High-resolution sensor measurements are streamed in real time to the DT platform, enabling continuous model synchronization, validation, and performance assessment under realistic EV operating scenarios.

Voltage prediction accuracy is evaluated using a comprehensive set of performance metrics, including the root mean squared error (RMSE) and mean absolute error (MAE) to quantify overall prediction precision, and the coefficient of determination (R²) to assess goodness of fit. In addition, peak transient error is used to measure the model’s ability to capture fast voltage dips and spikes during dynamic events, while algorithm execution latency is recorded to ensure the proposed method meets real-time implementation requirements.
(23)
RMSE =
1
N
∑
k = 1
N
(
V
k
−
V

(24)

1

(25)

∑

2

V
̅
)
2
These equations jointly define a self-correcting, ageing-aware, and physics-consistent DT framework capable of ultra-short-term voltage prediction (50–500 ms) under dynamic EV conditions.

The performance of the proposed framework is benchmarked against several widely used battery modeling and estimation approaches. Specifically, comparisons are conducted with traditional ECM–based BMSs, estimators relying solely on EKF, and purely data-driven artificial intelligence models, including LSTM, conventional neural network, and GRU architectures. This comparative evaluation highlights the advantages of the proposed hybrid DT–based approach in terms of prediction accuracy, robustness, and adaptability under dynamic operating conditions.

The proposed measurement platform combines multiple sensing modalities—including high-resolution voltage and current sensors, distributed temperature arrays, and periodic low-amplitude impedance probing—to ensure comprehensive monitoring of battery behavior. Since these sensing channels operate at different sampling rates and response times, a multilayer temporal synchronization mechanism is implemented to maintain consistency between the physical battery measurements and the DT model.

First, all sensing modules are referenced to a common time base generated by the central data acquisition controller, which provides precise timestamping of each measurement sample. High-frequency signals such as voltage and current are sampled at a higher rate to capture fast electrical transients during dynamic EV driving conditions, while temperature measurements and impedance probing operate at lower sampling frequencies appropriate for slower thermal and electrochemical dynamics.

Second, a time-alignment and resampling layer is employed in the data preprocessing stage. In this stage, lower-rate measurements (e.g., temperature and impedance) are interpolated or temporally aligned with the high-frequency voltage–current measurement timeline. This ensures that the DT receives a synchronized multisensor state vector at each update step.

Third, the DT update cycle is governed by an event-driven synchronization loop, where voltage and current measurements trigger rapid updates of the electrical model, while slower variables such as temperature are incorporated through periodic correction steps. This hierarchical update structure improves reliability while avoiding unnecessary computational overhead.

Finally, the sensor-fusion algorithm integrates the synchronized measurements to update the DT states and feed the short-term prediction module. By maintaining consistent timestamps and coordinated sampling intervals across heterogeneous sensing channels, the framework ensures reliable DT synchronization even during high-frequency sampling and rapid load transitions. These additional explanations have been incorporated into the revised manuscript to clarify how the proposed instrumentation framework maintains reliable and temporally consistent DT updates.

The proposed DT architecture employs an EKF to estimate internal battery states such as state-of-charge (SOC), internal resistance variation, and polarization voltages. During transient current loads, the battery system exhibits rapid electrical dynamics that may introduce measurement noise and modeling uncertainty. To ensure stable estimation performance under these conditions, several design considerations were implemented.

First, the state-space model used in the EKF is derived from the electro-thermal ECM, which captures the dominant battery dynamics while maintaining a reduced-order structure suitable for real-time implementation. This physically grounded model improves filter stability by limiting divergence caused by unmodeled dynamics.

Second, the process and measurement noise covariance matrices are carefully tuned using experimental datasets collected under dynamic driving cycles. This tuning allows the filter to adaptively balance trust between model predictions and sensor measurements, preventing instability when large transient currents occur.

Third, the framework incorporates high-frequency voltage and current sensing, which provides sufficient temporal resolution to accurately capture rapid current fluctuations during acceleration events. The availability of dense measurement updates allows the EKF to correct prediction errors quickly, thereby maintaining estimation stability.

In addition, a residual monitoring mechanism is implemented within the DT synchronization loop. When the innovation (measurement residual) exceeds a predefined threshold during extreme transients, adaptive covariance adjustment is applied to temporarily increase measurement weighting. This strategy improves robustness against sudden disturbances without compromising long-term filter stability.

Fig. 5 illustrates the proposed DT–enabled estimation architecture based on the EKF for LiB monitoring in EV applications. The framework integrates an electro-thermal ECM with high-frequency sensing of voltage, current, and temperature to enable accurate internal state estimation. The EKF performs multisensor fusion to estimate key battery variables such as SOC, internal resistance, and polarization voltages, while adaptive tuning of process and measurement noise covariance improves robustness during transient load conditions. In addition, a residual monitoring mechanism enables dynamic covariance adjustment to maintain estimation stability during rapid current variations associated with EV acceleration and regenerative braking. Experimental validation using representative driving cycles confirms the capability of the proposed approach to achieve stable and accurate state estimation under highly dynamic operating conditions.

Finally, experimental validation using representative EV driving profiles—including aggressive acceleration segments—demonstrated that the proposed filtering framework maintains stable and bounded estimation errors for voltage and SOC prediction, confirming its suitability for real-time DT–enabled BMS applications. These clarifications have been incorporated into the revised manuscript to further highlight the robustness and stability of the proposed Kalman filter–based estimation strategy under highly dynamic operating conditions typical of EVs ( Fig. 6).

The hardware platform used to validate the proposed DT–enabled voltage prediction framework consists of a high-precision multisensor measurement chain integrated with a real-time processing unit. As shown in Fig. 7 (Hardware Setup Block Diagram), the LiB pack is interfaced with 3 primary sensing modules: a high-accuracy voltage sensing circuit, a bidirectional current shunt or Hall-effect sensor, and a distributed temperature-monitoring array. These sensors provide raw signals representing instantaneous electrical and thermal states of the cell or module. The analog signals are routed to a multichannel high-resolution ADC,ensuring synchronized sampling under dynamic load conditions. The use of low-noise printed-circuit board routing, differential signal paths, and isolated transducer interfaces minimizes measurement distortion and enhances the fidelity of the input data fed into the DT.

After analog-to-digital conversion, the digitized data streams are transmitted to the embedded controller or edge computing unit responsible for executing the real-time DT model. The processing platform integrates the lithium-ion ECM, electro-thermal ageing estimators, and AI-based short-term voltage prediction algorithms. The DT continuously compares predicted outputs with physical sensor readings, automatically recalibrating the model parameters to compensate for sensor drift, temperature variations, and ageing effects. The resulting predictions and diagnostic indicators are then communicated to the BMS, enabling enhanced state estimation, early fault detection, and predictive control decisions. This hardware configuration provides the foundation for a high-fidelity closed-loop DT ecosystem that significantly improves voltage prediction accuracy, safety, and EV battery performance.

The proposed DT–enabled measurement and prediction framework was experimentally validated using the hardware setup shown in Fig. 7. The system was tested under 3 representative EV loading conditions: (i) dynamic current pulses, (ii) UDDS urban drive cycle, and (iii) aggressive acceleration–regenerative braking transients. Across all test scenarios, the synchronized high-bandwidth sensing platform provided stable, low-noise voltage, current, and temperature measurements, enabling seamless real-time updates of the DT model. The hybrid prediction engine—combining the ECM and LSTM-based short-term forecasting—demonstrated strong accuracy in predicting voltage dynamics, especially during rapid current fluctuations where traditional BMS estimators typically exhibit significant error. Measurement data confirmed that integrating impedance-based calibration and sensor fusion reduced drift-related deviations, resulting in consistent agreement between predicted and measured terminal voltages.
Tables 1–6 summarize the principal parameters used to configure the proposed DT–enabled battery measurement and instrumentation framework. Table 1 presents the time discretization and prediction settings, including the sampling interval, simulation duration, and short-term voltage prediction horizon. Table 2 lists the fundamental electrical characteristics of the LiB cell used in the study, while Table 3 provides the parameters of the ECM representing the battery’s dynamic electrical behavior. The OCV–SOC relationship used for voltage estimation is detailed in Table 4, defining the mapping between SOC and OCV across the full operating range. In addition, Table 5 summarizes the specifications of the sensing instrumentation, including voltage, current, and temperature sensor accuracy, drift, and bandwidth characteristics. Finally, Table 6 presents the thermal model parameters, which capture heat generation, thermal resistance, and heat transfer dynamics, enabling accurate electro-thermal representation within the DT framework.

To further assess the broader applicability of the proposed framework, its expected performance under demanding operating conditions such as high-rate charging and elevated thermal environments was also considered. The integration of the electro-thermal battery model with high-frequency voltage, current, and temperature sensing enables the DT to capture rapid electrical and thermal dynamics during HPC events. In addition, the temperature-dependent parameter adaptation and Kalman filter–based sensor fusion allow the system to continuously correct model states under elevated thermal stress. These capabilities enable the framework to maintain stable voltage prediction and reliable state estimation even under aggressive charging profiles and high-temperature conditions, highlighting its potential applicability in fast-charging EV infrastructures and high-performance battery systems. The configuration parameters used in the proposed DT framework are summarized in
Tables 7–12. Table 7 presents the key hyperparameters of the AI-based voltage prediction model, including the LSTM architecture, input features, and training settings. Table 8 defines the current profile parameters used to emulate realistic EV driving conditions during simulation, while Table 9 lists the adaptive algorithm parameters governing SOC estimation, model calibration, and resistance adaptation. Table 10 establishes the performance evaluation criteria for voltage prediction accuracy, SOC estimation, and prediction horizon. Furthermore, Table 11 outlines the update rates of different components within the DT architecture to ensure real-time synchronization, and Table 12 summarizes the computational requirements of each subsystem, demonstrating that the proposed framework maintains low processing overhead suitable for practical BMS implementations.

These parameter tables provide the complete specification for implementing the DT framework, ensuring ultra-accurate short-term voltage prediction while maintaining real-time performance suitable for EV BMS applications. Quantitative evaluation revealed substantial improvements over baseline BMS methods. In pulse-load tests, the DT achieved a prediction RMSE of 8–12 mV, outperforming classical observer-based models (25–40 mV) and standalone neural network predictors (18–25 mV). During drive-cycle testing, the prediction horizon of 50–300 ms ahead preserved a high correlation (>0.98) with ground truth measurements, enabling proactive identification of voltage dips and recovery phases. This predictive capability is especially critical in high-speed EV applications where fast decision-making is required to manage power flow, prevent over-discharge, and maintain inverter stability. Additionally, the framework demonstrated robustness against ageing effects: after introducing artificially aged cells, the DT automatically recalibrated its internal parameters, such as internal resistance and polarization time constants, thereby maintaining prediction accuracy without requiring retraining. This adaptability highlights the value of combining physics-based constraints with ML inference.

Thermal effects were also analyzed, revealing that the integrated temperature-sensing array contributed significantly to prediction accuracy during high-load events. When temperature compensation was disabled, voltage prediction error increased by up to 40%, confirming the necessity of multidomain sensing in EV environments. The impedance-probing subsystem further enhanced long-term stability by adjusting degradation indicators within the DT, improving SOH-informed prediction fidelity. Overall, the results clearly demonstrate that the proposed DT–enabled measurement system provides a resilient, adaptive, and highly accurate framework for short-term voltage prediction in lithium-ion EV batteries. The combination of advanced hardware instrumentation and hybrid modeling represents a significant advancement beyond traditional BMS capabilities, offering a reliable pathway toward predictive battery management, improved EV safety, and optimized battery utilization.

Figs. 8–11 present key results from the proposed DT–enabled voltage prediction framework. Fig. 8 presents the comprehensive performance evaluation of the proposed DT framework for battery voltage prediction. The top subplot demonstrates the close tracking between the actual and predicted voltage waveforms throughout the 60-second simulation period. The bottom subplot shows the prediction error over time, which remains predominantly within ±0.05 V bounds, indicating high prediction accuracy. Fig. 9 illustrates the SOC estimation performance, where the DT maintains accurate tracking of the true SOC. Finally, the bottom histogram confirms that the prediction errors follow an approximately normal distribution centered near zero, validating the statistical properties of the estimation algorithm.

Fig. 10 provides a comparative analysis between the proposed DT approach and a conventional simple ECM-based prediction method over a representative 10-second window. The DT predictions (blue dashed line) exhibit significantly closer tracking of the actual voltage waveform (solid black line) compared to the simple ECM approach (red dotted line). The embedded text box quantifies this improvement, showing that the DT achieves an RMSE of [3.6607] V, representing a [improvement]% reduction in prediction error compared to the simple ECM's RMSE of [0.0199] V. This substantial improvement validates the effectiveness of the DT's adaptive mechanisms and AI-enhanced prediction capabilities for real-time battery management applications.

Fig. 11 presents a detailed analysis of sensor characteristics crucial for understanding measurement quality and DT performance. The left subplot reveals the voltage sensor drift over time, showing a cumulative drift of 642.86 mV with a drift rate of 1.07e-2 V/s, which must be compensated for accurate voltage estimation. The middle subplot displays the voltage noise distribution after drift removal, exhibiting a nearly Gaussian profile with a standard deviation of 3.34 mV, confirming that the sensor noise meets the expected statistical characteristics for high-precision measurements. The right subplot illustrates the relationship between battery current and temperature, color-coded by time, demonstrating the expected thermal response where elevated currents (both positive and negative) correlate with temperature increases due to internal power dissipation, validating the thermal model incorporated in the DT framework ( Table 13).

To substantiate these degradation metrics, the study utilizes long-term cycling datasets and periodic diagnostic measurements collected during battery operation under representative EV driving profiles. SEI growth is indirectly quantified through capacity fade trends and increases in irreversible lithium loss, which are commonly used indicators in LiB ageing studies. These parameters were extracted by analyzing the evolution of charge–discharge curves and coulombic efficiency during repeated cycling tests.

Lithium plating propensity is evaluated through low-temperature and high-current charging experiments, where voltage relaxation behavior and incremental capacity analysis are used to detect abnormal electrochemical signatures associated with lithium deposition. These experimental observations are incorporated into the DT ageing model as constraints affecting internal resistance and voltage dynamics.

Additionally, internal resistance evolution is experimentally verified using periodic impedance measurements and current pulse tests, which allow the estimation of ohmic and polarization resistance components throughout the battery life cycle. The measured resistance growth is then used to calibrate the ageing-related parameters within the electro-thermal model.

By integrating these experimentally derived indicators into the DT framework, the proposed model maintains consistency between measured degradation behavior and predicted ageing dynamics, thereby improving the reliability of long-term voltage prediction and state estimation. These additional clarifications have been incorporated into the revised manuscript to strengthen the credibility of the ageing model and its predictive capability.

This work presents a comprehensive DT–enabled measurement and prediction framework for LiB voltage estimation in EV applications. By integrating advanced high-frequency sensing instrumentation, hybrid physics–AI modeling, and real-time adaptive calibration, the proposed system overcomes key limitations of conventional BMS architectures, including sensor drift, nonlinear dynamics, and evolving ageing effects. The DT combines an enhanced ECM, thermal representation, and ageing mechanisms to form a continuously updated virtual replica of the physical battery. When coupled with multisensor instrumentation and Kalman filter–based sensor fusion, the system maintains high-fidelity state estimation even under aggressive transient loads, such as rapid acceleration or regenerative braking, ensuring stable and reliable voltage prediction.

The AI-based short-term prediction module further improves system performance by forecasting battery voltage 50–500 ms ahead using an LSTM/temporal model with adaptive learning. The framework’s design allows residual and diagnostic analysis, enabling explainable insights into potential degradation mechanisms—such as SEI growth, lithium plating, or thermal stress—and operational anomalies, providing actionable guidance for BMS control strategies and maintenance planning.

Experimental and simulation studies across representative EV driving cycles demonstrated that the DT consistently outperforms conventional BMS approaches in prediction accuracy, stability, and robustness against sensor noise and drift. The system’s ability to adaptively update model parameters in response to ageing and thermal variations highlights its scalability and applicability to large battery packs and demanding operating environments, including high-rate charging and elevated thermal conditions. These capabilities also allow better utilization of the battery’s safe operating window, enhanced power management, and improved safety margins.

Overall, this work establishes a scalable, interpretable, and intelligent foundation for predictive battery management, demonstrating that DT–assisted instrumentation can significantly enhance EV safety, efficiency, and operational reliability. Future work will extend hardware-in-the-loop validation, explore integration with fast-charging scenarios, and incorporate PINNs for deeper insight into electrochemical and degradation phenomena, further strengthening both predictive capability and diagnostic interpretability.

Ahmed G. Abo-Khalil: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mahmoud Aref: Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Khairy Sayed: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. El-Zohri Hebatallah H.: Data curation, Formal analysis, Resources, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
